1: Servants of Christ and stewards of God's mysteries: apostolic identity is Christologically defined by downward roles, not upward status. Hypēretai Christou (servants of Christ): the apostles are functionaries of Christ, not independent agents. Oikonomoi mystēriōn Theou (stewards of God's mysteries): the mysteries are the Christological plan disclosed in the gospel — apostles are entrusted with the announcement of what God has done in Christ, not the inventors of it.
2: Faithfulness is the steward's requirement: apostolic accountability is to Christ, not to audience approval. The faithful steward delivers the master's goods intact and correctly. Applied to apostolic ministry: the gospel of Christ crucified must be delivered without the distortion of rhetorical self-display, cultural accommodation, or factional loyalty. Faithfulness to Christ is the single criterion.
3: Human evaluation is a very small thing; even self-evaluation is unreliable: it is the Lord who judges. The eschatological displacement of human judgment to Christ as the ultimate evaluator removes the power of the Corinthian assessment of Paul. Christ's judgment on the Last Day is the only verdict that counts — and that verdict will be made by the one who was himself crucified as a criminal and raised as Lord.
4: A clear conscience does not guarantee divine acquittal: the ultimate standard is Christ's evaluation, not the conscience's verdict. The conscience can be uninformed or self-deceived; Christ as judge has perfect knowledge of both actions and motives. Paul's detachment from human evaluation is grounded in his acceptance of Christ as the only evaluation that matters.
5: Judge nothing before the appointed time — wait until the Lord comes. The Lord's coming (elthē ho Kyrios) is the Parousia of Christ when hidden things will be exposed. The eschatological judgment is not punitive terror but revelatory clarity: every person's work will be fully seen and fairly evaluated by the one who himself knows what it is to suffer unjust human judgment. Praise will come from God — the ultimate honor-award belongs to Christ to give, not to the Corinthians to bestow.
6: Paul applied these things to himself and Apollos for the Corinthians' benefit: the apostolic examples are pedagogical models of Christological humility. The principle me hyper ha gegraptai (nothing beyond what is written) grounds apostolic authority in Scripture — neither Paul nor Apollos has authority beyond what the Christ-centered Scripture establishes. Puffed-up faction-building is ruled out by the Christological ground of all apostolic ministry.
7: What do you have that you did not receive? The rhetorical question dismantles every form of Corinthian self-congratulation. Gifts of wisdom, eloquence, spiritual experience — all received from God through Christ. This is the anti-boasting argument in its simplest form: the cross defines Christian identity as entirely gift-received, never self-achieved. Those who have Christ's Spirit receive everything; there is nothing to boast in.
8: Already satisfied, already rich, already reigning — without us! Paul's irony exposes the Corinthians' over-realized eschatology. The kingdom has not yet fully come; Christ has not yet returned; the resurrection has not yet transformed the body. To act as if all eschatological blessings are already in hand is to skip the cross-shaped present — the suffering-with-Christ that precedes glorification-with-Christ (Romans 8:17). The apostles' ongoing suffering (1 Corinthians 4:9-13) is the present Christological reality, not the triumphalism the Corinthians have appropriated prematurely.
9: God has put the apostles on display as those condemned to death — a spectacle for the whole cosmos. The apostolic peristasis is not incidental suffering but Christological conformity: as Christ was put on display at the cross as a condemned criminal, so the apostles are put on display as weak, dishonored, and condemned. The cross-pattern is not limited to one moment in history but continues in the apostolic body until the Parousia.
10: We are fools for Christ (mōroi dia Christon): the fool-vocabulary from 1 Corinthians 1:18-25 is now applied to Paul's own apostolic experience. The wisdom of the cross is manifested in the community of the cross-shaped: those who have accepted the foolishness of the crucified Christ as their framework have become fools by the standards of the present age. The contrast with the Corinthians' self-perception — wise, strong, honored — exposes their cross-avoidance.
11: Hunger, thirst, nakedness, brutality, homelessness: the peristasis catalogue as embodied Christology. Christ himself experienced poverty (2 Corinthians 8:9), homelessness (Matthew 8:20), and rejection by his own people (John 1:11). Apostolic hardship is not random suffering but participation in the cross-pattern of the Lord who gave himself. Paul's suffering is not evidence against Christ's power but evidence of conformity to Christ's cruciform existence.
12: Working with our own hands; when cursed, we bless; when persecuted, we endure; when slandered, we answer kindly: the behavioral pattern of the crucified Christ in apostolic existence. Christ blessed those who cursed him (Luke 23:34), endured those who persecuted him (Hebrews 12:3), and answered his accusers with truth rather than retaliation (Matthew 26:63-64). The apostle's cross-shaped responses are not mere ethical choices but Christological conformity — Paul is showing what it looks like to live the cross.
13: We have become the scum of the earth, the garbage of the world: the nadir of social status as the Christological place of apostolic existence. Christ was treated as a criminal and hanged outside the city (Hebrews 13:12-13) — the ultimate social rejection. Paul's position as peripsēma (scum/refuse) is the apostolic continuation of the cross's social shame. Those who follow the crucified Christ must be willing to share his social location.
14: I write this not to shame you but to warn you as my dear children: Paul's fatherly relationship with the Corinthian community is itself grounded in the gospel. His concern is not self-promotion but their formation in Christ. The cross-shaped apostle calls for cross-shaped community life — the warning is pastoral, not punitive, from one who bears the cross's marks (cf. Galatians 6:17).
15: You may have many guardians in Christ but not many fathers — I fathered you in Christ through the gospel. Paul's unique founding relationship with the Corinthian community is a spiritual paternity through Christ: the gospel that brought them to life in Christ is the same gospel Paul preached (en Christō Iēsou dia tou euaggeliou egennēsa). The life they have in Christ flows through the apostolic proclamation of Christ.
16: Become imitators of me: not self-aggrandizement but Christological pedagogy. The call to imitate Paul is grounded in Paul's own imitation of Christ (1 Corinthians 11:1: be imitators of me as I am of Christ). The chain of imitation — community imitates apostle, apostle imitates Christ — is the Christological transmission mechanism for cross-shaped living. The community learns cruciformity by watching it embodied in apostolic existence.
17: Timothy as faithful representative in the Lord: the chain of Christological transmission extends through co-workers. Timothy will remind them of Paul's ways in Christ — the cross-shaped pattern is not location-specific but universal (as I teach everywhere in every church). The Christological pattern of weak, cross-bearing apostolic ministry is the same curriculum for all communities.
18: Some have become arrogant as if Paul were not coming: the arrogance is a form of cross-avoidance. The puffed-up faction-leaders have used Paul's absence to assert their own status — exactly the patron-seeking, status-climbing behavior that the cross rules out. The arrogance is ultimately a refusal of the cross's verdict on human self-promotion.
19: Paul will come and find out not the talk of the arrogant ones but their power: the cross-criterion applied to ministry evaluation. Talk (logos) without Spirit-power is the Sophistic mode the cross has rendered obsolete. Paul will evaluate the Corinthian leaders by the same standard Christ applied to him — not rhetorical performance but Spirit-enabled transformation. The Lord willing: the eschatological qualification acknowledges that Christ is Lord over all apostolic plans.
20: The kingdom of God is not a matter of talk but of power: the eschatological kingdom that Christ inaugurated is demonstrated by Spirit-power, not rhetorical display. This is the Christological summary of the entire argument of 1 Corinthians 1-4: the crucified Christ is God's power (1 Corinthians 1:24), not human eloquence. The kingdom he established and will consummate is present and visible in Spirit-enabled transformation of lives, not in Sophistic wisdom-performances.
21: Shall I come with a rod of discipline or in love and a spirit of gentleness? The two modes of apostolic presence both reflect aspects of Christ: the rod of the father who disciplines (Hebrews 12:6-7; the Lord disciplines those he loves) and the gentleness of the one who is meek and humble in heart (Matthew 11:29). The Corinthians' response to this letter will determine which Christological mode of apostolic presence they receive.
